military_service.unit: Co. K, 14th Regt. Inf., Conn. Vols., 2d Army Corps, Army of the Potomac
military_service.notes: Enlisted as Private August 7, 1862. Wounded May 10, 1864 at the Battle of Laurel Hill, Virginia (during the Spotsylvania Court House campaign). Recovered and served until discharge September 7, 1865. The 14th Connecticut was part of the Army of the Potomac's 2d Army Corps and saw heavy action throughout the Overland Campaign.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his paternal uncle Alfred Cowles (H01139), the lawyer who later migrated to California.

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded at Spotsylvania in the Overland Campaign
narrative_blocks.body: A Hartford clerk and grocer who enlisted in 1862, he took a wound at the Battle of Laurel Hill in May 1864 during the Spotsylvania fighting, then served to the war’s end. He finished his long life farming at Willington, dying at eighty-eight.

burial.plot_notes: Cowles-Wheeler Cemetery East Willington Tolland County CT (FG #2147876). Same cemetery as her husband Alfred II — and the Cowles-Wheeler name reflects her Wheeler maternal line.

parents.father_research_notes: Mr. Hill of Willington CT. Cowles book #1688 leaves first name blank. Not built per Jalapeño.
parents.mother_research_notes: Miss Wheeler. Mother's name (née Wheeler) per Cowles book; first name not given.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Harriet 'Harty' M. Hill Tourtelotte Cowles (I00390).

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Woodruff' for his paternal grandmother Sophia Sarah Woodruff Cowles (I00383). Carries the Woodruff family name to a fourth generation in the Egbert line.

notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut state senator (1848), legislator, and probate judge

cross_connections.display_label: His son's father-in-law — Massachusetts disestablishment champion.

documents.document_blurb: Egbert Cowles's 1880 book 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony' — written at age 95, self-taught.

institutions.role: Stockholder and supporter — Morning Star mission steamer (1884)
institutions.notes: Took stock in the Morning Star steamer; carved its axe helve.

military_service.notes: Quartermaster, 7th Connecticut Militia Cavalry, 1810-1812 (just prior to/at outbreak of War of 1812).

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Large farmer in Farmington CT, living in the lower part of the town.

career.title: Member, Connecticut State Legislature
career.notes: Represented Farmington 1832.

career.title: Justice of the Peace
career.notes: 24-year tenure.

career.title: Judge of Probate Court
career.notes: Judge of the Probate Court for many years per Cowles book.

career.title: Author
career.notes: At age 95 published 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony; Historical, Biographical and Anecdotical' (DOC019). Self-taught with limited formal schooling.

career.title: Congregational Church member
career.organization: First Church of Christ Congregational, Farmington CT
career.notes: Approximately 80 years of membership in the Farmington Congregational Church (joined ~1804 as a young adult). Urged establishment of a Sunday school at Scott's Swamp, an outer Farmington district.

career.title: American Board of Foreign Missions supporter
career.organization: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
career.notes: ABCFM was organized in Farmington in 1810 when Egbert was twenty-five. He took stock in the Morning Star mission steamer (launched ~1884 for Micronesian missionary work) and personally carved an axe helve for the cabin of the new vessel.

narrative_blocks.header: Society planned his hundredth birthday celebration
narrative_blocks.body: The Farmington Ecclesiastical Society arranged a celebration for Egbert Cowles's 100th birthday — but he died February 27, 1885, five weeks shy of the planned April 4 occasion. He had served as a Connecticut state senator, state legislator, deputy sheriff, justice of the peace for twenty-four years, and probate judge. 'A town or society meeting for a long series of years which did not listen to his addresses of wisdom was unknown.'

narrative_blocks.header: Five children died young, six survived
narrative_blocks.body: Egbert and Sophia had eleven children between 1813 and 1831 in Farmington Connecticut. Five died before age thirteen, with replacement-naming patterns cycling through Edgar, Arthur, and Lucius across multiple deceased siblings. Six survived to adulthood: Esther Rachel, Lucius Samuel, John Egbert, Alfred II, Arthur D., and Edgar Philip — including John Egbert who lived to meet Wilford Woodruff in Salt Lake City sixty years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Authored a Plymouth Colony history at ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: At age 95 in 1880 — self-taught with only district-school education — Egbert published 'Sketches of the Early Settlements of the Plymouth Colony; Historical, Biographical and Anecdotical'. Contemporaries praised the book's clear concise style; Egbert had spent his hundred years in Farmington except for one Southern winter, two or three trips to Ohio, and a single journey to St. Louis.

narrative_blocks.header: Carved the Morning Star steamer's axe helve
narrative_blocks.body: Egbert was twenty-five when the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) was organized in Farmington in 1810; he remembered Governor Treadwell and Dr. Noah Porter framing the new society. Seventy-four years later in 1884 he took stock in the Morning Star mission steamer bound for the Micronesian Isles, and personally carved a beautiful axe helve for the new vessel's cabin.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington Hartford County CT (FG cemetery #2098376). The historic Old Burying Ground also called Memento Mori. CEM not built — only confirmed Hooker connection so far is Egbert.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Woodruff of Southington CT (not built per Jalapeño). Cowles book gives Woodruff descent: Samuel → Samuel → Samuel → Samuel → Matthew. NOTE: Hinman attributed her father as 'Hon. Samuel Woodruff of Windsor CT, judge of the County Court, agent to Greece, published European travels' — Cowles book corrects this attribution to Samuel + Esther Sloper of Southington.
parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Sloper Woodruff of Southington CT (not built per Jalapeño).

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived six of her eleven children
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia bore eleven children with Egbert in Farmington Connecticut between 1813 and 1831. Five died before age thirteen, with replacement-naming patterns cycling through Edgar, Arthur, and Lucius across multiple deceased siblings; she also outlived a sixth child Arthur D. who died at twenty-nine in 1857. Sophia herself died at age 85 in August 1874, eleven years before Egbert reached his near-century.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.
Revolutionary grandfather; she pioneered California through her marriage.
Both Hooker descendants involved with Tunxis affairs, 1738 and 1804.

military_service.notes: Battle of Long Island Jul 1776; Saratoga and SURRENDER OF BURGOYNE Oct 17, 1777; under Col. Noadiah Hooker 1779; Sergeant-Major 1779; clerk to Lt.-Col. Austin 1781. Maj.-Gen. Parsons recommended him to Washington for commission, May 20, 1782 (Library of Congress, Washington Papers, Vol.3, p.2122). Pensioned 1832.

Revolutionary War service per pension application: July 1776 under Capt. John Porter to NY (in Battle of Long Island, retreated to Harlem Plains and over King's Bridge to White Plains; regiment disbanded Sept 28, 1776); 1777 under Capt. Ichabod Norton (attacked fort at Kingsbridge, defeated, discharged); September 1777 joined Capt. Ichabod Norton commanding regt. of 4 cos. 15th Conn. Militia, proceeded to Saratoga, present at the decisive action of Oct 7 and at the surrender of Burgoyne Oct 17, 1777; 1779 under Capt. Elisha Scott Col. Noadiah Hooker to New Haven; served as Sergeant-Major under Major Ichabod Norton; September 1781 drafted for three-week tour to New Haven in Lt-Col. Aaron Austin's regt., taken into Col. Austin's family as clerk/secretary. Pensioned under Act of June 7, 1832 commencing March 4, 1831, paid to death August 4, 1850.

documents.document_blurb: 1804 Connecticut General Assembly Resolve appointing Ezekiel Cowles Jr. as Overseer of the Farmington Tunxis Indians (replacing Capt. John Porter).

career.title: Member, Connecticut State Legislature
career.notes: Represented Farmington in the State Legislature.

career.title: Overseer of the Tunxis Indians
career.notes: Appointed by the Connecticut Legislature in 1804 as overseer and advisor of the Tunxis (Farmington Indians), replacing Capt. John Porter (under whom Ezekiel had served in 1776). The position made him one of a series of state-appointed Anglo-American managers of what remained of the Tunxis people's affairs and lands during a period of dispossession.

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Farmer at Farmington Connecticut throughout adult life.

narrative_blocks.header: Recommended to Washington for a commission
narrative_blocks.body: On May 20, 1782 Maj.-Gen. Samuel Holden Parsons wrote to General Washington recommending Sergeant-Major Ezekiel Cowles for a subaltern commission. Ezekiel had been at Long Island, Harlem Plains, and the surrender of Burgoyne; the war ended before the commission could be granted. The Parsons letter is preserved at the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: Appointed overseer of the Tunxis in 1804
narrative_blocks.body: In 1804 the Connecticut General Assembly appointed Ezekiel Cowles Jr. overseer and advisor of the Tunxis Indians at Farmington, replacing Capt. John Porter — under whom Ezekiel had marched to the Battle of Long Island in 1776. The role placed him in the same Tunxis-management chain as Justice John Mix, who signed the 1802 Sue Squa land deed and the 1816 Titus emancipation.

narrative_blocks.header: Four overlapping offices across twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Ezekiel filled four overlapping public offices across twenty-five years in Farmington: State Tax Collector (1794-1798), Deputy Sheriff (1802-1814), State Legislator (1807-1810), and Justice of the Peace (1809-1818). His 1850 estate inventoried at $6,549.12 — a substantial farmer's wealth in mid-century Connecticut.

sources.short: Cowles Family Genealogy

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Woodruff of Farmington CT (Ensign Samuel³, Matthew², Matthew¹) per Cowles book #303. Not built per Jalapeño.
